s: Venetian palace refused until an architect out-doge'd the Corporation
w: James Templeton wanted a carpet factory here; the Corporation kept saying no. He hired William Leiper to build something so extravagant it couldn't be refused: a replica of the Doge's Palace in Venice. In 1889, during construction, the ornate facade collapsed in a gale, killing 29 women in the weaving sheds below. It opened in 1892. Now WEST brewery. Bodies and brickwork.
_facts: Designed by William Leiper, modelled on the Doge's Palace in Venice, opened 1892.
Built for James Templeton and Son for manufacturing Axminster carpets.
On 1 November 1889 during construction, the facade collapsed killing 29 women in adjacent weaving sheds.
Listed as a Category A building; converted to Templeton Business Centre 1984, lifestyle village 2005.
Now houses WEST brewery, bar and apartments.

s: The world's largest terracotta fountain, a gift after an 1888 empire pageant
w: At 46 feet high and 70 feet wide, this is the world's largest terracotta fountain. Henry Doulton had it made for the 1888 International Exhibition of Science, Art and Industry to celebrate Queen Victoria's Golden Jubilee. Colonial figures from Canada, Australia, India and South Africa surround the base. Moved to its current spot in front of the People's Palace in 2005. Empire in fired clay, on a park older than the parliament.
_facts: World's largest terracotta fountain at 46 ft high and 70 ft across.
Gifted to Glasgow in 1888 after the International Exhibition of Science, Art and Industry by Sir Henry Doulton.
Commemorates Queen Victoria's Golden Jubilee; figures represent Canada, Australia, India and South Africa.
Extensively refurbished and moved to its position in front of the People's Palace in 2005.

s: The Second City in stone and murals — free tours of the Banqueting Hall
w: Queen Victoria inaugurated this Beaux-Arts monument in 1888. The rooftop figure of Truth is nicknamed 'Glasgow's Statue of Liberty' for its resemblance to New York's. Inside, a 33-metre Banqueting Hall painted by the Glasgow Boys hosted Nelson Mandela's Freedom of the City ceremony in 1993. The floor mosaic in the entrance hall encodes four Glasgow legends from Saint Mungo: bird, tree, bell, fish. Tours run Monday to Friday.
_facts: Designed by William Young; construction started 1882, inaugurated by Queen Victoria in August 1888.
The rooftop figure of Truth is nicknamed 'Glasgow's Statue of Liberty'.
The 33.5-metre Banqueting Hall features murals by the Glasgow Boys, coordinated by William Leiper.
Nelson Mandela received the Freedom of the City here in 1993; Sir Alex Ferguson in 1999.
The entrance floor mosaic dates from the 1950s and depicts four Saint Mungo emblems.

s: The pub claimed 1755; Johnson and Boswell really did sleep here
w: The original Saracen's Head Inn opened in 1755 — this is the fourth building, dating from 1904. The first did host Samuel Johnson and James Boswell for two nights in October 1773; Johnson enjoyed the coal fire. A Robert Burns IOU bearing the Tennent brewery seal is said to still be owned by the house. Billy Connolly set his 1974 monologue 'The Crucifixion' here. Only half the 'established 1755' sign is true.
_facts: Four establishments have stood here since 1755; the current building opened in 1904 at 209 Gallowgate.
Samuel Johnson and James Boswell stayed here 28-29 October 1773 during their Scottish tour.
A Robert Burns IOU bearing the Tennent brewery seal is said to be owned by the house.
A story of Johnson and Adam Smith having a swearing match here was disproved by John Wilson Croker.
Billy Connolly's 1974 monologue 'The Crucifixion' is set in this pub.

s: Gothic canopy for the man who ended Glasgow's cholera epidemics
w: In 1859, Lord Provost Robert Stewart piped fresh water from Loch Katrine, 56 km away, ending the cholera outbreaks that had killed thousands in Glasgow's courts. This Gothic fountain in Kelvingrove Park commemorates him. An engineer declared the aqueduct worthy to 'bear comparison with the most extensive aqueducts in the world, not excluding those of ancient Rome'. The water system runs still. Glasgow's finest fountain and almost nobody knows the story.
_facts: Built to commemorate Lord Provost Robert Stewart (1851-1854) who brought fresh water from Loch Katrine.
The Loch Katrine aqueduct opened in 1859 and ended cholera outbreaks in Glasgow.
The fountain is the largest monument in Kelvingrove Park.
An engineer compared the aqueduct to 'the most extensive aqueducts in the world, not excluding those of ancient Rome'.
The Milngavie water works, still operated by Scottish Water, supply Glasgow today.

s: 143-day occupation — longest seizure of a public building in British history
w: Glasgow's last Edwardian public bathhouse, opened 1912-1917, was closed by the council in 2001. Residents occupied it for 143 days — the longest occupation of a public building in British history. Local rumour holds the pools served as a WWII temporary mortuary for bombing casualties. The exterior was restored in 2023 but the baths await further funding. The ghost of the Edwardian 'steamie' is intact inside.
_facts: Glasgow's last surviving Edwardian public bathhouse, opened between 1912 and 1917, designed by A.B. McDonald.
Closed by Glasgow City Council in 2001; the 143-day occupation is the longest of a public building in British history.
Local rumour says the pools were used as a temporary mortuary for World War II bombing casualties.
The wash house or 'steamie' at the rear was converted to a launderette in 1971.
External refurbishment completed 2023; internal restoration awaits further funding.

s: Victorian trapeze rings hang over the pool — members only since 1876
w: Founded 1876 and never left Cranworth Street, this is one of two surviving Victorian private pools in Glasgow. Period trapeze and exercise rings still hang over the water; the Victorian Turkish bath is one of only three left in Scotland. In the 1970s the slated roof over the pool partially collapsed — the club almost died. A secretary fought to save it. In 2026 it celebrates 150 years. Unassuming door, extraordinary Victorian interior.
_facts: Founded in 1876; remains at 12 Cranworth Street, Hillhead — one of Britain's oldest continuously operating private pools.
Retains its original period trapeze and exercise rings over the swimming pool.
The Victorian Turkish bath is one of only three remaining in Scotland.
Nearly closed in the 1970s after the slated roof over the pool partially collapsed.
Category A listed building; founding member of Historic Pools of Britain.

s: Britain's largest aqueduct in 1790 — sail 62 feet above the River Kelvin
w: When this 445-foot stone aqueduct opened in 1790 it was the largest in Britain, carrying the Forth and Clyde Canal over the River Kelvin on four 50-foot arches, 62 feet above the water. Spectators watched sailing boats crossing above their heads and were astonished. It cost £8,509 — considerably over the £6,200 budget. Designed by Robert Whitworth; still navigable after the canal's restoration in 2001.
_facts: Designed by Robert Whitworth; opened 1790; Britain's largest aqueduct when built.
445 feet long, four arches of 50-foot span, 62 feet above the River Kelvin.
Cost £8,509 — over the original estimate of £6,200.
Spectators were impressed at the sight of sailing boats crossing above their heads.
Category A listed building; still carries boats after the Forth and Clyde Canal reopened in 2001.

s: Alexander Thomson's strangest church — Egyptian tower, Hindu columns
w: Built 1857-1859 by Alexander 'Greek' Thomson — who never visited Greece — this church on Blythswood Hill fuses Ancient Greek, Egyptian and Levantine forms into something uniquely his own. The tower draws from Egyptian sources; the interior columns read as Hindu. Thomson never left Scotland yet defined Glasgow's Victorian skyline. Category A listed. Mackintosh got the fame; Thomson built it first.
_facts: Designed by Alexander 'Greek' Thomson (1817-1875); built 1857-1859 for the United Presbyterian Church of Scotland.
Category A listed; Thomson fused Greek, Egyptian, and Levantine architectural forms.
Thomson never visited Greece.
The tower derives from Egyptian sources; interior columns have Hindu influences.
Now in Glasgow City Council care; used by a Free Church of Scotland Gaelic congregation since 1971.

s: Tiffany's spiritual forefather filled this church with glass and frescoes
w: Built 1865-1866 by William Leiper, this Gothic Revival church on Hyndland Street was filled with frescoes and stained glass by Daniel Cottier — the Glasgow-born designer credited as a profound influence on Louis Comfort Tiffany and with introducing the Aesthetic Movement to America and Australia. The congregation disbanded 1984. A charity trust renamed it for Cottier. The summer beer garden under the carved Gothic arches draws more visitors than the church ever did.
_facts: Built 1865-1866 by William Leiper; formerly Dowanhill United Presbyterian Church.
Contains frescoes and stained glass by Daniel Cottier (1838-1891), who influenced Louis Comfort Tiffany.
Cottier introduced the Aesthetic Movement to America and Australia.
The congregation merged with East Partick in 1984; the Four Acres Charitable Trust converted it to a theatre.
Category A listed; operates as music venue, theatre, bar and restaurant.

s: Category A ruin — listed two days before the scheduled demolition
w: The Reid family of Hyde Park Locomotive Works funded this cast-iron glasshouse in 1899-1900 on Springburn Park. Storm damage closed it in 1983. Glasgow District Council planned demolition. On 22 March 1985 — two days before the bulldozers — the Scottish Office granted it Category A listed status. It has stood as a maintained ruin ever since, trees growing through the ironwork, community trust slowly campaigning for restoration.
_facts: Constructed 1899-1900 by Simpson & Farmer of Partick; funded by the Reid family of Hyde Park Locomotive Works.
Closed after storm damage in 1983; Glasgow District Council planned demolition.
Granted Category A listed status on 22 March 1985 — two days before planned demolition.
Steel came from the Temple Ironworks at Anniesland and Glengarnock Steelworks.
Maintained as a Category A listed ruin; Springburn Winter Gardens Trust campaigns for restoration.

s: 40-foot cast iron showpiece, gifted to Glasgow after the 1901 Exhibition
w: Walter Macfarlane's Saracen Foundry in Possilpark shipped cast ironwork to every corner of the British Empire. For the 1901 Glasgow International Exhibition he built this 40-foot fountain inspired by the Choragic Monument of Lysicrates in Athens. After the exhibition closed he presented it to the city. Originally in Kelvingrove Park, it was moved to Alexandra Park, where its cast dragons and cherubs still gleam in the east end.
_facts: Made by Macfarlane & Co.'s Saracen Foundry for the 1901 Glasgow International Exhibition; gifted by Walter Macfarlane.
40-foot cast-iron fountain; design influenced by the Choragic Monument of Lysicrates in Athens.
Originally sited in Kelvingrove Park; later moved to Alexandra Park.
Decorated with cast dragons at the top and cherubs on the rim.
Category A listed building.

s: Thomson's finest villa — historians say it may have shaped Frank Lloyd Wright
w: Completed in 1858 for paper manufacturer James Couper, Holmwood is Alexander 'Greek' Thomson's most elaborate residential design — one of very few to survive with its original interior. The dining room frieze reproduces Flaxman's illustrations of Homer's Iliad. When published in 1868, the design was cited as a possible influence on Frank Lloyd Wright. National Trust for Scotland; Thomson's polychromatic colour schemes are being restored.
_facts: Completed 1857-1858 for James Couper, a paper manufacturer; designed by Alexander 'Greek' Thomson.
Considered Thomson's finest residential design; the dining room frieze uses Flaxman's illustrations of Homer's Iliad.
The published design in 1868 is cited as a possible influence on Frank Lloyd Wright.
Category A listed building; owned and managed by the National Trust for Scotland.
Rare Thomson building retaining original interior polychromatic decoration.

n: St Mungo Museum of Religious Life and Art
s: Britain's only religion museum and Britain's first Zen garden
w: Opened in 1993 on the site of Glasgow's medieval Bishop's Castle, this is the only museum in the British Isles dedicated solely to religion. Its Zen garden — Britain's first — was designed by a Japanese Zen expert and won a Japanese Foundation award. The stained glass came from redundant Glasgow churches. Shortly after opening a fundamentalist attacked one of the exhibits. The sandstone courtyard is the most unexpected quiet spot in the city centre.
_facts: Opened 1993; the only museum dedicated to religion and art in the British Isles.
Built on the site of Glasgow's medieval Bishop's Castle; constructed 1989 in Neo-Scottish baronial style.
Stained glass sourced from redundant Glasgow churches.
Houses Britain's first Zen garden, designed by a Japanese Zen expert and awarded by the Japanese Foundation.
Exhibits subject to controversy; a Christian fundamentalist attacked one exhibit shortly after opening.

s: 1649 statues of charity brothers, transplanted onto an 1802 facade
w: Brothers George (c.1580-1639) and Thomas Hutcheson bequeathed money for an elderly hospital and a school for poor boys; their school still operates, now as Hutchesons' Grammar School. David Hamilton's 1802-1805 building incorporated stone statues of the brothers carved in 1649 from the demolished 1641 original on Trongate. You can stand in the Merchant City and look up at men who were charitable in Glasgow before the Merchant City existed.
_facts: George Hutcheson (c.1580-1639) and Thomas Hutcheson (1589-1641) bequeathed money for elderly care and a school for poor boys.
Current building designed by David Hamilton, constructed 1802-1805 as Hutchesons' Hospital.
The facade incorporates two statues of the Hutcheson brothers carved by James Colquhoun in 1649, from the demolished 1641 Trongate building.
Hutchesons' Grammar School, from the brothers' bequest, still operates as a fee-paying independent school.
Category A listed building; restored in 2014 as a restaurant.
